title: Faisal Islam: What people in power think the impact of the Iran war will be
summary:
While there is still much uncertainty from the Gulf, the clouds are less dark now, allowing some to focus on other concerns.
Reeves could even be hopeful after growth figures showed the UK on course for 0.5% to 0.6% growth in the first quarter.
As news emerged yesterday of the reopening of the Strait, energy prices tumbled.
So too did borrowing costs, petrol prices, and mortgage rates.

title: How Will Markets React to Strait of Hormuz Being Closed Again?
summary:
After the announcement of the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz on Friday, the Dow went up nearly 900 points, and the S&P 500 and Nasdaq also rose sharply.
It comes as oil prices also plunged which might mean gas prices will start coming down as well.
NBC’s Allie Canal joins Saturday TODAY to break down what Iran’s new closure of the Strait of Hormuz means for the economy, gas prices and more.

title: An expert forecasts how the Iran war could hit your budget
summary:
According to March’s Consumer Price Index (CPI), food prices were no higher last month than they had been in February.
The war with Iran has yet to produce any discernible increase in food prices.
So, it takes time for an energy shock to work its way through the supply chain.
So the impact of rising diesel costs may not work its way into that part of the supply chain for weeks.
So, when they pass through the supply chain, consumers can substitute: If beef gets more expensive, they can eat more chicken.

title: Iran says it has closed the Strait of Hormuz again, as ceasefire nears its end
summary:
Speaking to reporters in the Oval Office Saturday, Trump said Iran had tried to get "cute" in taking back control of the Strait of Hormuz.
Despite hinting at the possibility of resuming attacks on Iran, Trump also told reporters "I think it's going to happen," referring to a deal.
Hezbollah, meanwhile, urged people displaced by the fighting not to rush back to southern Lebanon, the Bekaa Valley and Beirut's southern suburbs until the situation becomes clearer.
The latest round of fighting began after Hezbollah fired rockets into northern Israel following the U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran on Feb. 28.
Israeli forces responded with airstrikes and a ground invasion of southern Lebanon.

title: I've lived in China for over 10 years and saw the OpenClaw craze unfold. The West needs to keep up.
summary:
This as-told-to essay is based on a conversation with Thomas Derksen, a German entrepreneur who has lived in China for over a decade.
There's so much happening in China, and I'm always interested in how things are different in the West and in Asia.
People in China are more curious, and they have a strong business mindset.
Some people don't think normal jobs will give them a good future, so they try OpenClaw to build something.
In China, a crayfish shop owner sees a tool and thinks: Maybe I can use this to grow my business.

title: Ben Roberts-Smith: Australia's most decorated living soldier released on bail over war crimes charges
summary: If Roberts-Smith remained in custody, he would not be able to access or store such sensitive information in a secure environment, which would "seriously compromise his right to a fair trial", Howell said.

title: US To Create High-Tech Manufacturing Zone In Philippines
summary: A budget is just a method of worrying before you spend money, as well as afterward.

title: North Korea launches ballistic missiles toward sea
summary:
North Korea launches ballistic missiles toward seatoggle caption AP/KCNA via KNSSEOUL, South Korea — North Korea launched multiple ballistic missiles toward the sea on Sunday, its neighbors said, the North's latest weapons testing activity this year.
South Korea's Joint Chiefs of Staff said the launches happened on Sunday morning from the North's eastern Sinpo area.
It said South Korea has bolstered its surveillance posture and is closely exchanging information with the U.S. and Japan.
It said Tokyo strongly protested to Pyongyang, saying Sunday's launches threaten regional and international peace and violated U.N. Security Council resolutions that bans any ballistic activities by North Korea.
Last week, North Korea said leader Kim Jong Un supervised missile tests from the country's destroyer.

title: The Strait of Hormuz opens for commercial ships but the U.S. blockade continues on Iran
summary:
The Strait of Hormuz opens for commercial ships but the U.S. blockade continues on Irantoggle caption Ibrahim Amro/AFP via Getty ImagesIran said it has reopened the Strait of Hormuz for commercial ships, after Israel and Hezbollah began a temporary ceasefire overnight.
First, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the passage for commercial ships through the Strait of Hormuz was "completely open" on a coordinated route previously indicated by Iran.
Sponsor MessageIran's Foreign Ministry spokesperson, Esmail Baghaei, told Iranian state outlet Mehr that if the naval blockade continues, Iran will take reciprocal action.
Trump also said Iran was removing "all sea mines," with the help of the U.S., presumably referring to mines placed in the Strait of Hormuz.
Oil prices tumbled and stock markets rallied on the announcement the strait was open for commercial ships.

Rumeysa Ozturk, the Tufts University student and Palestinian rights advocate who was arrested by masked federal agents in March 2025 has voluntarily returned to her native Turkey after completing her Ph.D. and reaching a settlement agreement with the Trump administration, the ACLU announced Friday.

title: Israel starts a tense ceasefire in Lebanon. And, Trump nominates a new CDC director.
summary:
Israel starts a tense ceasefire in Lebanon.
Today's top storiesA 10-day ceasefire to pause fighting between Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah militants in Lebanon begins today.
President Trump announced the deal yesterday on social media after he had separate phone calls with leaders of Israel and Lebanon.
Iran has said it would not negotiate peace with the U.S. unless Israel entered a ceasefire in Lebanon.
❓ Quiz: This newsletter has kept you updated on the latest Iran war news.

title: Pakistan PM, army chief wrap up key trips in push for more US-Iran talks
summary:
Field Marshal Asim Munir leaves Tehran while premier Shehbaz Sharif heads home from Turkiye amid hopes of another round of US-Iran talks.
Munir held talks with the country’s president, foreign minister, parliament speaker and head of Iran’s military central command centre.
But diplomacy has continued, with Pakistan’s Prime Minister, Shehbaz Sharif, visiting Saudi Arabia, Qatar and Turkiye to push the peace process.
Donald Trump has said a second round of talks with Iran could be held in Pakistan in the coming days.
Reporting from Islamabad, Al Jazeera’s Kamal Hyder said Munir landed back home on Saturday as Pakistan prepared for another round of US-Iran talks expected “within the next few days”.

title: Iran war: What is happening on day 50 of the US-Iran conflict?
summary:
The Strait was reopened earlier on Saturday subject to conditions, in line with the Lebanon ceasefire and previously coordinated maritime routes.
In the GulfAdvertisementFragile recovery: Saudi Finance Minister Mohammed Al-Jadaan welcomes Iran reopening the Strait of Hormuz, but says the situation remains fragile.
In IsraelIsrael ‘prohibited’ from striking Lebanon: Trump says Israel is barred by the US from continuing attacks on Lebanon, declaring “enough is enough”.
Lebanon war ‘last anti-colonial battle’: Analyst Rami Khouri says the conflict reflects resistance by Iran-aligned groups against decades of Western influence, with southern Lebanon at its core.
Boeing’s war boost: The war has so far boosted Boeing’s defence business and hasn’t affected deliveries to airline customers facing high jet fuel prices, the company’s CEO said.

title: Some ships are sailing through the Strait of Hormuz. Others are turning around. Here's what 'completely open' looks like for now.
summary:
Iran announced Friday morning that the Strait of Hormuz is open for maritime traffic, and mid-afternoon data showed that commercial vessels were passing through the strategic waterway, though only a small number.
MarineTraffic data from Friday afternoon showed that just over half a dozen commercial vessels had transited the strait since the announcement.
Data from KPler, a real-time trade intelligence platform, showed that eight commercial vessels had crossed as of 2 pm EST Friday compared to five on Thursday.
But by the evening, new data showed ships were turning back.
Earlier in the week, days before the announcement on the strait, US Navy warships set up a blockade of Iranian ships and ports.

title: World reacts to Strait of Hormuz reopening amid US-Iran conflict
summary:
Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi and United States President Donald Trump have said that the Strait of Hormuz is open to commercial vessels.
Trump affirmed on social media that the strait was open, later claiming that Iran had agreed to “never close the Strait of Hormuz again”.
He said he wanted US involvement in the international mission to secure shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
FinlandFinnish President Alexander Stubb, who participated in the Paris summit, said on X, “We welcome Iran’s announcement on opening the Strait.
Since the outbreak of the conflict, we have followed the guidance of our security partners in the region, and the recommendation so far has been to avoid transiting the Strait of Hormuz.

title: This Ukrainian counter-drone company is worried about missing its moment in the Iran war
summary:
A Ukrainian interceptor drone taking flight.
Much of Ukraine's defense industry says exports would actually boost domestic production.
One Ukrainian defense industry leader told Business Insider that interceptor exports could ultimately mean more interceptors for Ukraine's military.
He added that joint production with allied countries — already growing — could benefit both partners and Ukraine's war effort.
A Ukrainian interceptor drone, which typically costs $6,000 or less, is a more appealing and effective option.

title: Iran foreign minister says Strait of Hormuz ‘completely open’
summary:
Iran says ‘passage for all commercial vessels through Strait of Hormuz’ open during the Israel-Lebanon ceasefire.
Iran has said the Strait of Hormuz is “completely open” for all commercial vessels and will remain so during the Israel-Lebanon ceasefire, as United States President Donald Trump said an agreement to end the US-Israeli war on Iran was “very close”.
“In line with the ceasefire in Lebanon, the passage for all commercial vessels through Strait of Hormuz is declared completely open for the remaining period of ceasefire,” Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said in a post on X on Friday.
He said in a separate post that Iran had agreed to “never close the Strait of Hormuz again”.
The conflict saw Iran block traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas transits.

title: Iran war: What is happening on day 49 of the US-Iran conflict?
summary:
Celebrations in Lebanon as a 10-day ceasefire begins, opening the door to talks on a longer-term Israel-Lebanon deal.
Trump said an agreement to end the war on Iran is near, with the next round of talks possibly this weekend in Islamabad.
‘No sticking points’ in Iran talks: The US president said a deal is “very close” and could be “very good for everybody”.
Trump hopes Hezbollah ‘acts nicely’: Trump said on Thursday he hopes Hezbollah “acts nicely and well” during the 10-day truce.
Hezbollah in ceasefire: Trump said the truce between Israel and Lebanon would include Iran-backed Hezbollah.

title: Turkiye woos investors amid Iran war fallout in Gulf economies
summary:
Turkish officials are promoting Istanbul as a regional financial hub amid the war’s fallout on the Gulf economies.
As the fallout of the war has reverberated across the Middle East, Ankara has jumped at the chance to promote Turkiye as a model of security and stability for businesses and investors.
“A liberal investment environment, ease of entry and new comprehensive incentive packages should help boost its position,” Bagis told Al Jazeera.
“There is a particularly strong strategic focus from Far Eastern institutions,” the spokesperson told Al Jazeera.
“In emerging financial systems, investor confidence is primarily driven by predictability, transparency,” Dincer told Al Jazeera.
